main_summary.summary: William Lamar Strickland, interviewed by Joe Mosnier at New Africa House, University of Massachusetts Amherst on September 23, 2011, traces a movement biography that begins in Boston's Roxbury and extends through national Black radical politics. Born January 4, 1937, Strickland attended Boston Latin School and Harvard, served in the Marine Corps from 1956 to 1959 (including a posting in Albany, Georgia), and returned to Harvard for graduate work with Erik H. Erikson. He recounts a childhood acquaintance with Malcolm Little through his cousin Leslie, who shared time with Malcolm at Charlestown State Prison, and a later reconnection with Malcolm X at Harvard and in Harlem. Strickland joined the Northern Student Movement under founder Peter Countryman and succeeded him as executive director, expanding city projects in Boston, Hartford, New York, Detroit, and Chicago. He worked with William Kunstler and Arthur Kinoy organizing congressional support for the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party's 1965 challenge brought by Fannie Lou Hamer, Victoria Gray Adams, and Annie Devine, with Representative William Fitts Ryan introducing the floor resolution. Strickland joined Vincent Harding at the Institute of the Black World in Atlanta, taught at UMass Amherst, served as chief consultant on the PBS Malcolm X documentary Make It Plain, and discusses Frantz Fanon, Tanzania, Walter Rodney, and the Black Power conferences.
key_themes: Malcolm X's critique of American racism
Northern Student Movement and community organizing
Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party congressional challenge
Institute of the Black World and Black Studies
Vincent Harding and the convergence of Martin and Malcolm
main_summary.historical_significance:
Strickland's testimony documents the institutional architecture of Northern Black movement work that is often subordinated in standard SNCC- and SCLC-centered narratives: the Northern Student Movement's tenant organizing with Jesse Gray in Harlem, the legal-congressional substrate of the MFDP challenge organized with William Kunstler and Arthur Kinoy, and the founding of the Institute of the Black World in Atlanta as the first independent Black think tank in U.S. history. His firsthand recollections of Malcolm X in Roxbury, Harlem, and at Harvard, and his account of the Charlestown State Prison overlap between his cousin Leslie and Malcolm Little, provide primary-source corroboration of personal-history details that recent biographies have contested.

The interview also preserves Strickland's documentary record as chief consultant on the PBS Make It Plain (1994) and as a writer who responded publicly to Manning Marable's 2011 Malcolm X biography, situating him as a custodian of Malcolm X interpretive scholarship across four decades. His reflections on Vincent Harding's evolving role at the King Center, the development of Black Studies curricula, and his Marine Corps formation as a Black serviceman in the late 1950s Jim Crow South add structural detail to multiple parallel threads in the Civil Rights History Project corpus.

chapters.title: Family, Roxbury, and Malcolm Little
chapters.summary: Strickland describes his upbringing in Roxbury after his father was killed in the Italian campaign of World War II, his mother's work at Raytheon, and summer visits to relatives near Macon, Georgia, where his ancestor Madison Gray had left land to the family. He recounts meeting Malcolm Little as a child through his cousin Leslie, who served time at Charlestown State Prison concurrently with Malcolm, and reconnecting with Malcolm X years later after a Harvard speaking event.

chapters.title: Boston Latin and Harvard
chapters.summary: Strickland recounts entering Boston Latin School at the elementary recommendation of a teacher and graduating with six Black classmates from a starting class of nearly 700. He arrived at Harvard with only eleven Black undergraduates in his cohort of a thousand, learned writing discipline from his teacher Mr. Russo, and was told by the small Black Harvard community that his obligation was to demonstrate genius rather than nepotism.

chapters.title: NAACP Youth and Marine Corps
chapters.summary: Strickland describes his NAACP Youth Council involvement during high school under the slogan Free by '63 and the Emancipation Proclamation centennial. He enlisted in the Marine Corps from 1956 to 1959, expected an Albany New York assignment, and discovered en route to Howard University that his orders were to Albany, Georgia. He describes Marine Corps culture as a primarily Southern institution that taught reserves of endurance while exposing him to dimensions of white America he otherwise would not have encountered.

chapters.title: Awakening at Harvard
chapters.summary: Returning to Harvard after the Marine Corps, Strickland recalls Emmett Till's murder as the event that crystallized consciousness for many of his generation and remembers reading Richard Wright's Uncle Tom's Children in a course taught by Howard Mumford Jones, alongside James Baldwin's Notes of a Native Son. He recounts the Charles Street AME Church of his Boston childhood and notes how race was a context rather than a stated theme of his family life.

chapters.title: Northern Student Movement and Jesse Gray
chapters.summary: Strickland succeeded Peter Countryman as executive director of the Northern Student Movement, which operated city projects in Boston, Hartford, New York, Detroit, and Chicago and was connected to SNCC. He worked with Harlem tenant organizer Jesse Gray during East Harlem rent strikes, learning organizing as the practice of convincing people to organize themselves. He recounts meeting James and Grace Boggs in Detroit and connecting with Detroit Black organizers including the attorney Kenny Cockrel and General Baker.

chapters.title: Malcolm X in Harlem
chapters.summary: Strickland describes Malcolm X's ubiquitous presence on 125th Street during the early 1960s, his rallies near the Hotel Theresa, and his reading habits that included William F. Buckley's National Review. He recounts Malcolm X's critique of the sit-in tactic, his analysis of the Birmingham 1963 rebellion after the bombing of the A.G. Gaston Motel and A.D. King's house, and Malcolm X's observation in the Eyes on the Prize episode that the Civil Rights Act involved no new law.

chapters.title: Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party Challenge
chapters.summary: Strickland recounts the Freedom Vote organized in 1963 by Robert Parris Moses with Amzie Moore, the MFDP nomination of Fannie Lou Hamer, Victoria Gray Adams, and Annie Devine to challenge three white Mississippi congressmen, and the legal work conducted with William Kunstler and Arthur Kinoy of the National Lawyers Guild. He describes Representative William Fitts Ryan's resolution in January 1965 that forced the three white congressmen to step aside during debate, with 50 to 60 other representatives rising in support and the final vote reaching 149.

chapters.title: Mrs. Hamer and Malcolm X's Death
chapters.summary: Strickland describes sitting in Fannie Lou Hamer's home and recognizing that her clarity exceeded his Harvard education, identifying her alongside Malcolm X and Katherine Dunham as figures of irreplaceable wisdom. He recalls hearing of Malcolm X's assassination on February 21, 1965 at an NSM project directors meeting in upstate New York, and reflects that Malcolm X embodied the Black urban North in the way Martin Luther King embodied the South.

chapters.title: Black Heritage Series and Fanon
chapters.summary: Strickland describes producing the CBS Black Heritage: A History of Afro-Americans series with Vincent Harding and John Henrik Clarke, an early televised representation of Black Studies that CBS broadcast in early morning slots. He discusses his graduate work with Erik H. Erikson at Harvard, his discovery of Frantz Fanon through movement reading circles, and his shift from Freudian psychoanalysis toward Fanon's liberation psychology after the field moved from psychoanalysis to drug therapy.

chapters.title: Selma and Re-evaluating King
chapters.summary: Strickland arrived for the third Selma march and was filmed in the Eyes on the Prize Bridge to Freedom episode getting off the NSM bus. After King turned the marchers around in what was later revealed as a negotiated deal, Strickland's view of King declined, but his subsequent work in Atlanta with Vincent Harding and engagement with David Garrow's Bearing the Cross convinced him that King was converging toward Malcolm X's analysis in the final years before his assassination.

chapters.title: Institute of the Black World
chapters.summary: Strickland describes the Institute of the Black World's founding in Atlanta with Vincent Harding after a split from the King Center over the demand that researchers pledge nonviolence. He recounts visiting Walter Rodney at the University of Dar es Salaam in Tanzania around 1971-72, debating Stokely Carmichael over the All-African People's Revolutionary Party, and the recruitment of IBW faculty including Howard Dodson, George Napper, Pat Daly, and Jan Douglass. IBW operated as the first independent Black think tank in U.S. history until 1983, with Strickland identifying Malcolm X as its fundamental political influence.

chapters.title: Make It Plain and the Marable Critique
chapters.summary: Strickland describes serving as chief consultant on the PBS Eyes on the Prize episode The Time Has Come and on Blackside's subsequent Malcolm X documentary Make It Plain, for which he wrote the companion book. He summarizes his published critique of Manning Marable's 2011 Malcolm X biography, identifying historical omissions, unverified speculation under the reinvention trope, and unattributed borrowings from Karl Evanzz and Make It Plain itself.

chapters.title: SDS, White Organizers, and Political Analysis
chapters.summary: Strickland recounts his 1964 decision to remove white women from NSM ghetto organizing after observing the social dynamics of mattress-room wine-drinking sessions and trips to wealthy donor Peter Buttenwieser's home, while keeping white male organizers including Frank Joyce in Detroit. He closes with a political analysis covering the Obama election, ALEC, corporate tax avoidance, surveillance of Malcolm X by FBI, CIA, MI5, and Mossad, the Jersey City Black Panther deaths, Malcolm X's planned March 1965 trip to Algeria, and his projected memoir Once Upon a Time.
related_events: Malcolm X planned Algeria trip March 1965

loc_video.caption: William Lamar Strickland oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Amherst, Massachusetts, 2011 September 23
